steps.dialogue: Last lesson, you learned that some fractions equal exactly one whole. Remember how [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=8 denominator=8]eight eighths[/fraction] both landed at 1?
Quick check - where would [fraction numerator=5 denominator=5]five fifths[/fraction] go?

step.dialogue: Remember the pattern - when numerator equals denominator, the fraction equals 1.

metadata.description: Highlight position 1 on the number line

step.dialogue: [fraction numerator=5 denominator=5]Five fifths[/fraction] means all 5 parts out of 5. That's one whole.

metadata.description: Animate placing the point at position 1

step.dialogue: Let me help: [fraction numerator=5 denominator=5]five fifths[/fraction] has matching numbers, so it equals 1. Place it right here.

on_correct.dialogue: Correct. When the top and bottom numbers match, the fraction equals 1. You've got that pattern locked in.

steps.dialogue: You figured out that different fractions can equal the same whole number - like how [fraction numerator=3 denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=6 denominator=6]six sixths[/fraction] both equal 1. Today, we're going to explore how different fractions can equal the SAME amount even when they DON'T equal 1.

For example, [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=3 denominator=6]three sixths[/fraction] are actually the same amount. We'll explore this concept together so that it really sinks in. You're building on your understanding of fractions, step by step.

In our last lesson, we worked on fractions equaling one. Today, we'll explore another pattern in math - how different-looking fractions can be equal. Let's explore equivalent fractions - fractions that look different but are actually the same amount. We'll use our pattern-finding strategies from last time to build on our fraction knowledge today.
